'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '和歌山県田辺市本宮町本宮', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:力侍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11398393}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Rikishi Shrine |Image = [[Image:Rikishi_Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Approach to Rikishi Shrine |Location = {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}} Kawabe, aza Inai 61 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank/status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} claimed shrine (ronsha) / formerly {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} |Founded = Traditionally: mid-{{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} (kokerabuki) |Other name = |Pilgrimage site etc. = |Annual festival = October 10 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Rikishi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Rikishi Shrine'''|力侍神社}} is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kawabe, {{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}}, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}. Beginning with its所在地 of Kawabe, it attracts faith across the area as the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655|general ujigami}} for Ueno, Shima, Konami, and Kusumoto. It stands side by side east–west with Hachioji Shrine, which is a {{jalink|sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}}. There is also a theory that it is the successor shrine to “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” of Muro District, Kii Province in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} ({{jalink|ronsha|Q11064932|disputed Shikinaisha}}). It was formerly a {{jalink|village shrine|Q712534|former village shrine}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|modern shrine rank system}}. The {{jalink|main hall|Q2044994}} is designated a Tangible Cultural Property (structure) of Wakayama Prefecture together with that of Hachioji Shrine. The precincts are designated a Wakayama Prefecture historic site as the remains of {{jalink|Kawabe Ōji|Q11370042|Kawabe Ōji}}, one of the {{jalink|Kumano Kujūku Ōji||Kumano 99 Ōji}}. === Enshrined deities === It enshrines {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Mikoto|Q849412}} and {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|Q3090037}},“Nanki Jinja Roku” and “Meisho Zue” list the enshrined deity as **Asai-hime-no-Mikoto**. and additionally enshrines 13 other kami. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was originally enshrined at Ōyunohara (the former shrine site of {{jalink|Kumano Hongū Taisha|Q705035}}) in {{jalink|Hongū Town (Wakayama Prefecture)|Q2251838|Hongū Town}}, {{jalink|Tanabe City|Q304266}}, Wakayama Prefecture,{{Wiki coordinates dms|33|50|04|N|135|46|28|E|scale:25000}} but it is said that its founding began when, in the mid-{{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, it was transferred to Kuwahata in the aza of Konami, northeast of the present shrine site. “{{jalink|Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964}}” (hereafter abbreviated as “Zoku Fudoki”) argues that the “Jūshii-i-jō Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami” among the 30 local deities listed in “{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Shinmyōchō|Q17210008}}” (hereafter “Kokuchō”) in Nagusa District refers to this shrine, that the “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” listed in Muro District of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō was its predecessor, and that from the deity name and exploits of Tajikarao, the shrine name “{{jalink|rikishi|Q2727289}}” arose, which then changed into “Rikishi” Shrine; however,Although “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” appears in Muro District in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, the Kokuchō for Muro District contains neither that shrine name nor even a similar shrine; thus it argues that “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” was moved from Muro District to Nagusa District after compilation of the Engishiki, and therefore was recorded in the Kokuchō as “Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami” under Nagusa District, and that this was also the predecessor of the present shrine. because it cannot be proven from historical sources, it has been judged to have weak evidentiary basis.Hashimoto, “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine.” According to shrine tradition, after its founding it was transferred to the site of Hachioji Shrine in Ueno, immediately west of Konami,{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|16|06.0|N|135|15|11.7|E|scale:25000}} and later, in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kan’ei|Q1192913}} (March {{jalink|1626|Q6831}}) in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, it was moved together with Hachioji Shrine to the present location by divine oracle; Hachioji Shrine came to be enshrined in parallel as a sessha on the east side (to the right as you face it).Zoku Fudoki records a similar tradition. However, as noted below, the raising of the ridgepole of the present main hall of Hachioji Shrine was in the 1st year of Kan’ei. “Meisho Zue” also records the tradition that the transfer to the present site was due to a divine oracle, but dates it later, to the Hōei era (early 18th century). Since then it was regarded as the {{jalink|ubusuna-gami|Q1392097}} of four villages—Kawabe, Kusumoto, Shima, and Konami. According to Zoku Fudoki, a subtemple of {{jalink|Ninna-ji|Q1202871}} called Enkyōzan Sannō-in, a branch temple of {{jalink|Shinjō-in|Q131226873}}, was placed in the precincts as the shrine’s {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755|bettō jingū-ji}} (later abolished). In the 6th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in the 43rd year ({{jalink|1910|Q2075}}) it merged nearby shrines. A small shrine in the former Konami Village thought to be a remnant shrine of the original enshrinement site was also merged at that time,“Wakayama Kenshi.” and likewise Kusumoto Shrine in Kusumoto, regarded as the former site of Nakamura Ōji (one of the 99 Ōji), and the Hachioji shrine in Ueno (a small shrine remaining at the former enshrinement site in Ueno, regarded as the remains of Kawabe Ōji) were also merged. === Shrine buildings === The main hall is an {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684|ikken-sha nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{jalink|kokerabuki||kokerabuki}} roofing, and is a Wakayama Prefecture-designated Tangible Cultural Property (see the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section below). === Shrines within the precincts === ;Sessha: Hachioji Shrine :Today it enshrines {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|Q462555}}, the {{jalink|Five male and three female deities}}, {{jalink|Yachimatahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Yachimatahime-no-Mikoto}} as principal deities, with {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508}} and 11 other kami enshrined additionally. As noted above, it was formerly enshrined in Ueno; citing the “Kan’ei-ki,”A compilation of reports ordered by the {{jalink|Kishū Tokugawa clan|Q11605046}} after entering the domain to investigate “shrines and temples, old traditions and historic sites” within Kii Province, compiled over roughly 50 years from Kan’ei to Kanbun. The compilation appears to have been carried out multiple times; “Kan’ei-ki” refers to the portion compiled in the Kan’ei era (Zoku Fudoki). Zoku Fudoki states that it was founded in the {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} era (early 9th century), served as the ubusuna-gami for seven villages (Kawabe, Kusumoto, Shima, Konami, Ueno, Bessho, and Ochiai), and held more than 15 {{jalink|chō (unit)|Q1185752}} of shrine land, but that all shrine lands were confiscated during {{jalink|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550}}’s {{jalink|Kishū campaign|Q10855721|subjugation of Kishū}} in the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} period and that old documents were lost at that time. Zoku Fudoki itself further argues: “Upon consideration, this shrine is Kawabe Ōji as recorded in the ‘Kumano Gokō-ki,’“Gotoba-in Kumano Gokō-ki.” The record of Emperor Go-Toba’s Kumano pilgrimage in the 1st year of Kennin ({{jalink|1201|Q5225}}), described in {{jalink|Fujiwara no Teika|Q378425}}’s diary “{{jalink|Meigetsuki|Q3007406}}.” Also called “Kumano Michi no Aida Guki.” and this is the Hachioji shrine mentioned by the monk {{jalink|Shinkei|Q1770663}} in the afterword of ‘Sasamegoto.’”Shinkei was a mid-{{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} monk from Tai-no-shō, which included Kawabe Village, and was also active as a renga poet. His work “Sasamegoto” is a treatise on renga; its afterword states that after returning home for the first time in 55 years, he stayed in seclusion at Kawabe Ōji and wrote it. The main hall, like that of the main shrine, is also a prefecture-designated Tangible Cultural Property (see the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section below). ;Massha: Kusunoki Shrine :It enshrines Kusunoki Daimyōjin and stands east of Hachioji Shrine within the tamagaki fence. It was originally enshrined at Kusumoto, aza Minami-no-kuchi in Wakayama City,{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|15|37.5|N|135|15|30.9|E|scale:25000}} and is seen as “Jūshii-i-jō Kusumoto Ōkami” in the Kokuchō,“Nanki Jinja Roku,” “Meisho Zue,” and Zoku Fudoki. Like “Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami,” “Kusumoto Ōkami” is among the 30 local deities of Nagusa District. and in early modern times it was called “Kusumoto Shrine” or “Kusumoto Daimyōjin.” Tradition holds that it was founded by {{jalink|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979}} in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} (786), after he exterminated a giant spider living in Kusumoto; it was made an unranked shrine in Meiji 6, but in Meiji 43 it was merged into Rikishi Shrine in the form of a transfer of enshrinement. Regarding this giant spider, “{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Meisho Zue}}” (first volume published in the 8th year of {{jalink|Bunka (era)|Q1373146|Bunka}} [{{jalink|1811|Q6928}}]; hereafter “Meisho Zue”) says that long ago in Kusumoto a huge {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}} towered, and a giant spider there spun a web from the camphor tree to Wasa-yama (present {{jalink|Takatsumi-yama}}),{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|14|05.31|N|135|15|40.8|E|scale:25000}} troubling the people; therefore, in the {{jalink|Daidō (Japan)|Q1373191|Daidō}} era (early 9th century), Tamura Shōgun (Tamuramaro), having received an {{jalink|imperial command}}, exterminated it and felled the camphor tree as well, and from the stump camphor trees grew in clusters, forming a forest; it further records that at that time (early 19th century) there was in fact a stump around 30 {{jalink|hiro|Q11458676}} (about 50 {{jalink|meters|Q11573}}) in circumference, thought to be several thousand years old. Zoku Fudoki identifies Kusumoto Shrine with “{{jalink|Nakamura Ōji site|Q135351770}}” among the 99 Ōji, and this is considered the current prevailing theory, though as noted below there are dissenting views (see “{{jalink|#Historic site Kawabe Ōji remains||Historic site Kawabe Ōji remains}}” in the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section). In addition, {{jalink|Sugihara Yasushige}} (the “{{jalink|Nanki Jinja Roku}}” of {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 3 [1746]) and “Meisho Zue” list the enshrined deity as {{jalink|Kumano Kusubi|Q11298796|Kumano Kusubi-no-Mikoto}}. In addition, there are subsidiary shrines: Ushidaki-sha (within the tamagaki fence), Inari-sha, and Myōken-sha. === Rituals === In early modern times, as noted above, a bettō temple was established, and the four “ubugo” villages also organized a {{jalink|miyaza|Q11454763}} that appears to date back to the medieval period, overseeing rituals, shrine construction, rebuilding, and transfers of the deity. The miyaza was operated around the “bandō” (said to preserve the name of the {{jalink|bandō|Q11578792}} placed in medieval {{jalink|shōen|Q1550557}} estates). The bandō formed a group called “Tsuitachi-kō,” holding meetings on the first day of the 1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, and 12th lunar months, where they made decisions broadly covering village governance as well as shrine management. Members other than the bandō were called “Satamori (Sato-no-mori)” and also “Hiraza.” At first these roles were filled based on old family rank and tradition supported by economic advantage, but as some lineages died out and others declined with socio-economic development, from the mid–early modern period onward new farmers were incorporated, distinguishing old lineages as “Reiza” and new entrants as “Hōbi-nyūza.” They were called “hōbi” mainly because contributions to the guild were required, and as this shows, from the mid-period onward finances became strained; by the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} era they even suspended the Tsuitachi-kō to save costs and reformed long-standing practices to stabilize finances, such as lending saved funds and using the interest as operating capital. Along with this, conflicts arose over seating order between Reiza and Hōbi-nyūza,A temporary resolution was reached in the 2nd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} (1862) by making Reiza the upper seat (“Kawabe Shōza Nai-za Chōsho,” dated the first month of that year). and conflicts also occurred between the guild and non-guild members, indicating a transitional state toward the {{jalink|ujiko|Q125562175|ujiko system}} that abolished privileges based on family status.Andō, “Historical Study of Early Modern Miyaza.” The aforementioned “Za Chōsho” is introduced in that book’s source appendix as “Kawabe Shō Documents (4).” Today, October 10 is the annual festival day, and this together with January 1; April 10; July 10; August 18; and December 10—six festivals per year—are referred to as “annual festival days.” === Cultural properties === (The type and date of designation are shown in parentheses after each item.) === Wakayama Prefecture designations === * Main hall (Tangible Cultural Property [structure], June 9, {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 54 [{{jalink|1979|Q2484}}]) * Main hall of the sessha Hachioji Shrine (same) :Both are ikken-sha nagare-zukuri structures with hiwada-buki roofing, decorated throughout with brilliant polychrome, with {{jalink|chigi and katsuogi|Q87220234}} on the ridge. Verandas ({{jalink|ōyuka}}) are provided on three sides—the front and both sides—with {{jalink|gibōshi|Q3105399}} balustrades; side screens ({{jalink|wakishōji}}) are installed; and the rear veranda is omitted. The front of the moya has sliding lattice doors ({{jalink|kōshi-do|Q11017436}}), while the other three sides are white-painted board walls; aside from these, the buildings are decorated in polychrome. From the {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}} it is known that the ridgepole-raising of the main shrine’s main hall was in Kan’ei 11, and that of the sessha main hall predates it, in Kan’ei 1; in addition to excellent carvings on the {{jalink|kibana}} and {{jalink|kamaboko (kaerumata)|Q123117911}} elements, the original form at the time of construction has been well preserved, and they are regarded as representative examples of shrine architecture in the prefecture that retains the legacy of the {{jalink|Momoyama period}}.Interpretive signboard titled “Rikishi Shrine Main Hall / Sessha Hachioji Shrine Main Hall,” dated March 31, Heisei 5, issued jointly by the Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education, Wakayama City Board of Education, and Rikishi Shrine.Architectural Institute of Japan (ed.), “Sōran Nihon no Kenchiku,” Vol. 6-II (Nara & Wakayama), Shinkenchiku-sha, 2002. The sessha main hall is built somewhat larger than the main shrine’s main hall; however, whereas the walls of the sessha’s moya are plain, the main shrine’s main hall has flowers painted on the walls of both side faces. Both were designated in Shōwa 54.{{Cite web|Japanese |author=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkenzo.html |title=Prefecture-designated Cultural Properties / Tangible Cultural Properties / Structures |accessdate=2009-10-25 }} * Kawabe Ōji remains (Historic site, April 1, Shōwa 33 [1958]) :Designated in Shōwa 33 as the “Kawabe Ōji remains,” the ruins of Kawabe Ōji, one of the Kumano 99 Ōji.{{Cite web|Japanese |author=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=Prefecture-designated Cultural Properties / Monuments |accessdate=2009-10-25 }} However, as the Kawabe Ōji remains, Zoku Fudoki identifies Ueno—the former site of Hachioji Shrine—as the location, and since “Wakayama-ken Seiseki” reaffirmed that view, that site is now considered a strong candidate (currently designated a Wakayama City historic site); moreover, there is also a theory that this shrine is the site where Nakamura Ōji was located, and many issues remain regarding the locations of the Ōji on the north bank of the {{jalink|Kinokawa River}} including Kawabe Ōji.{{jalink|Koyama Yasunori|Q11460013}} “Kumano Pilgrimage and the Pilgrimage Roads” (in “Wakayama City History,” Vol. 1, Wakayama City, Heisei 3; “Medieval Wakayama,” Chapter 1, Section 4, Item 1). === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Andō Seiichi, “Historical Study of Early Modern Miyaza,” Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, Shōwa 35 (1960) * Kishū Domain (ed.), “Kii Zoku Fudoki” (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Clergy Supervisory Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, Meiji 43 (1910) * Takaichi Shiyū, “Kii Meisho Zue” 1 (retitled reprint edition by Rekishi Tosho-sha), Rekishi Tosho-sha, Shōwa 45 (1970) * Hashimoto Kankichi, “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” (in Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), “Shikinaisha Survey Report,” Vol. 23, Nankaidō, Kōgakkan University Press, Shōwa 62 [1987], included) * “Wakayama Kenshi,” Vol. 2, Wakayama Prefecture, Taishō 3 (1914) * “Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture” (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), Heibonsha, 1983 (Shōwa 58) ISBN 9784582490312 * “Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place Names,” Vol. 30 (Wakayama Prefecture), Kadokawa Shoten, Shōwa 60 (1985) ISBN 4-04-001300-X * “Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer,” Wakayama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines, Heisei 7 (1995) === Related items === * {{jalink|Kumano Kujūku Ōji (Wakayama City)|Q11370042}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1019 Rikishi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines) {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kumano Hongū Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]

'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '和歌山県田辺市本宮町本宮', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:力侍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11398393}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Rikishi Shrine |Image = [[Image:Rikishi_Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Approach to Rikishi Shrine |Location = {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}} Kawabe, aza Inai 61 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank/status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} claimed shrine (ronsha) / formerly {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} |Founded = Traditionally: mid-{{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} (kokerabuki) |Other name = |Pilgrimage site etc. = |Annual festival = October 10 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Rikishi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Rikishi Shrine'''|力侍神社}} is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kawabe, {{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}}, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}. Beginning with its所在地 of Kawabe, it attracts faith across the area as the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655|general ujigami}} for Ueno, Shima, Konami, and Kusumoto. It stands side by side east–west with Hachioji Shrine, which is a {{jalink|sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}}. There is also a theory that it is the successor shrine to “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” of Muro District, Kii Province in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} ({{jalink|ronsha|Q11064932|disputed Shikinaisha}}). It was formerly a {{jalink|village shrine|Q712534|former village shrine}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|modern shrine rank system}}. The {{jalink|main hall|Q2044994}} is designated a Tangible Cultural Property (structure) of Wakayama Prefecture together with that of Hachioji Shrine. The precincts are designated a Wakayama Prefecture historic site as the remains of {{jalink|Kawabe Ōji|Q11370042|Kawabe Ōji}}, one of the {{jalink|Kumano Kujūku Ōji||Kumano 99 Ōji}}. === Enshrined deities === It enshrines {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Mikoto|Q849412}} and {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|Q3090037}},“Nanki Jinja Roku” and “Meisho Zue” list the enshrined deity as **Asai-hime-no-Mikoto**. and additionally enshrines 13 other kami. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was originally enshrined at Ōyunohara (the former shrine site of {{jalink|Kumano Hongū Taisha|Q705035}}) in {{jalink|Hongū Town (Wakayama Prefecture)|Q2251838|Hongū Town}}, {{jalink|Tanabe City|Q304266}}, Wakayama Prefecture,{{Wiki coordinates dms|33|50|04|N|135|46|28|E|scale:25000}} but it is said that its founding began when, in the mid-{{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, it was transferred to Kuwahata in the aza of Konami, northeast of the present shrine site. “{{jalink|Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964}}” (hereafter abbreviated as “Zoku Fudoki”) argues that the “Jūshii-i-jō Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami” among the 30 local deities listed in “{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Shinmyōchō|Q17210008}}” (hereafter “Kokuchō”) in Nagusa District refers to this shrine, that the “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” listed in Muro District of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō was its predecessor, and that from the deity name and exploits of Tajikarao, the shrine name “{{jalink|rikishi|Q2727289}}” arose, which then changed into “Rikishi” Shrine; however,Although “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” appears in Muro District in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, the Kokuchō for Muro District contains neither that shrine name nor even a similar shrine; thus it argues that “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” was moved from Muro District to Nagusa District after compilation of the Engishiki, and therefore was recorded in the Kokuchō as “Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami” under Nagusa District, and that this was also the predecessor of the present shrine. because it cannot be proven from historical sources, it has been judged to have weak evidentiary basis.Hashimoto, “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine.” According to shrine tradition, after its founding it was transferred to the site of Hachioji Shrine in Ueno, immediately west of Konami,{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|16|06.0|N|135|15|11.7|E|scale:25000}} and later, in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kan’ei|Q1192913}} (March {{jalink|1626|Q6831}}) in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, it was moved together with Hachioji Shrine to the present location by divine oracle; Hachioji Shrine came to be enshrined in parallel as a sessha on the east side (to the right as you face it).Zoku Fudoki records a similar tradition. However, as noted below, the raising of the ridgepole of the present main hall of Hachioji Shrine was in the 1st year of Kan’ei. “Meisho Zue” also records the tradition that the transfer to the present site was due to a divine oracle, but dates it later, to the Hōei era (early 18th century). Since then it was regarded as the {{jalink|ubusuna-gami|Q1392097}} of four villages—Kawabe, Kusumoto, Shima, and Konami. According to Zoku Fudoki, a subtemple of {{jalink|Ninna-ji|Q1202871}} called Enkyōzan Sannō-in, a branch temple of {{jalink|Shinjō-in|Q131226873}}, was placed in the precincts as the shrine’s {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755|bettō jingū-ji}} (later abolished). In the 6th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in the 43rd year ({{jalink|1910|Q2075}}) it merged nearby shrines. A small shrine in the former Konami Village thought to be a remnant shrine of the original enshrinement site was also merged at that time,“Wakayama Kenshi.” and likewise Kusumoto Shrine in Kusumoto, regarded as the former site of Nakamura Ōji (one of the 99 Ōji), and the Hachioji shrine in Ueno (a small shrine remaining at the former enshrinement site in Ueno, regarded as the remains of Kawabe Ōji) were also merged. === Shrine buildings === The main hall is an {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684|ikken-sha nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{jalink|kokerabuki||kokerabuki}} roofing, and is a Wakayama Prefecture-designated Tangible Cultural Property (see the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section below). === Shrines within the precincts === ;Sessha: Hachioji Shrine :Today it enshrines {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|Q462555}}, the {{jalink|Five male and three female deities}}, {{jalink|Yachimatahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Yachimatahime-no-Mikoto}} as principal deities, with {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508}} and 11 other kami enshrined additionally. As noted above, it was formerly enshrined in Ueno; citing the “Kan’ei-ki,”A compilation of reports ordered by the {{jalink|Kishū Tokugawa clan|Q11605046}} after entering the domain to investigate “shrines and temples, old traditions and historic sites” within Kii Province, compiled over roughly 50 years from Kan’ei to Kanbun. The compilation appears to have been carried out multiple times; “Kan’ei-ki” refers to the portion compiled in the Kan’ei era (Zoku Fudoki). Zoku Fudoki states that it was founded in the {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} era (early 9th century), served as the ubusuna-gami for seven villages (Kawabe, Kusumoto, Shima, Konami, Ueno, Bessho, and Ochiai), and held more than 15 {{jalink|chō (unit)|Q1185752}} of shrine land, but that all shrine lands were confiscated during {{jalink|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550}}’s {{jalink|Kishū campaign|Q10855721|subjugation of Kishū}} in the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} period and that old documents were lost at that time. Zoku Fudoki itself further argues: “Upon consideration, this shrine is Kawabe Ōji as recorded in the ‘Kumano Gokō-ki,’“Gotoba-in Kumano Gokō-ki.” The record of Emperor Go-Toba’s Kumano pilgrimage in the 1st year of Kennin ({{jalink|1201|Q5225}}), described in {{jalink|Fujiwara no Teika|Q378425}}’s diary “{{jalink|Meigetsuki|Q3007406}}.” Also called “Kumano Michi no Aida Guki.” and this is the Hachioji shrine mentioned by the monk {{jalink|Shinkei|Q1770663}} in the afterword of ‘Sasamegoto.’”Shinkei was a mid-{{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} monk from Tai-no-shō, which included Kawabe Village, and was also active as a renga poet. His work “Sasamegoto” is a treatise on renga; its afterword states that after returning home for the first time in 55 years, he stayed in seclusion at Kawabe Ōji and wrote it. The main hall, like that of the main shrine, is also a prefecture-designated Tangible Cultural Property (see the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section below). ;Massha: Kusunoki Shrine :It enshrines Kusunoki Daimyōjin and stands east of Hachioji Shrine within the tamagaki fence. It was originally enshrined at Kusumoto, aza Minami-no-kuchi in Wakayama City,{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|15|37.5|N|135|15|30.9|E|scale:25000}} and is seen as “Jūshii-i-jō Kusumoto Ōkami” in the Kokuchō,“Nanki Jinja Roku,” “Meisho Zue,” and Zoku Fudoki. Like “Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami,” “Kusumoto Ōkami” is among the 30 local deities of Nagusa District. and in early modern times it was called “Kusumoto Shrine” or “Kusumoto Daimyōjin.” Tradition holds that it was founded by {{jalink|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979}} in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} (786), after he exterminated a giant spider living in Kusumoto; it was made an unranked shrine in Meiji 6, but in Meiji 43 it was merged into Rikishi Shrine in the form of a transfer of enshrinement. Regarding this giant spider, “{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Meisho Zue}}” (first volume published in the 8th year of {{jalink|Bunka (era)|Q1373146|Bunka}} [{{jalink|1811|Q6928}}]; hereafter “Meisho Zue”) says that long ago in Kusumoto a huge {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}} towered, and a giant spider there spun a web from the camphor tree to Wasa-yama (present {{jalink|Takatsumi-yama}}),{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|14|05.31|N|135|15|40.8|E|scale:25000}} troubling the people; therefore, in the {{jalink|Daidō (Japan)|Q1373191|Daidō}} era (early 9th century), Tamura Shōgun (Tamuramaro), having received an {{jalink|imperial command}}, exterminated it and felled the camphor tree as well, and from the stump camphor trees grew in clusters, forming a forest; it further records that at that time (early 19th century) there was in fact a stump around 30 {{jalink|hiro|Q11458676}} (about 50 {{jalink|meters|Q11573}}) in circumference, thought to be several thousand years old. Zoku Fudoki identifies Kusumoto Shrine with “{{jalink|Nakamura Ōji site|Q135351770}}” among the 99 Ōji, and this is considered the current prevailing theory, though as noted below there are dissenting views (see “{{jalink|#Historic site Kawabe Ōji remains||Historic site Kawabe Ōji remains}}” in the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section). In addition, {{jalink|Sugihara Yasushige}} (the “{{jalink|Nanki Jinja Roku}}” of {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 3 [1746]) and “Meisho Zue” list the enshrined deity as {{jalink|Kumano Kusubi|Q11298796|Kumano Kusubi-no-Mikoto}}. In addition, there are subsidiary shrines: Ushidaki-sha (within the tamagaki fence), Inari-sha, and Myōken-sha. === Rituals === In early modern times, as noted above, a bettō temple was established, and the four “ubugo” villages also organized a {{jalink|miyaza|Q11454763}} that appears to date back to the medieval period, overseeing rituals, shrine construction, rebuilding, and transfers of the deity. The miyaza was operated around the “bandō” (said to preserve the name of the {{jalink|bandō|Q11578792}} placed in medieval {{jalink|shōen|Q1550557}} estates). The bandō formed a group called “Tsuitachi-kō,” holding meetings on the first day of the 1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, and 12th lunar months, where they made decisions broadly covering village governance as well as shrine management. Members other than the bandō were called “Satamori (Sato-no-mori)” and also “Hiraza.” At first these roles were filled based on old family rank and tradition supported by economic advantage, but as some lineages died out and others declined with socio-economic development, from the mid–early modern period onward new farmers were incorporated, distinguishing old lineages as “Reiza” and new entrants as “Hōbi-nyūza.” They were called “hōbi” mainly because contributions to the guild were required, and as this shows, from the mid-period onward finances became strained; by the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} era they even suspended the Tsuitachi-kō to save costs and reformed long-standing practices to stabilize finances, such as lending saved funds and using the interest as operating capital. Along with this, conflicts arose over seating order between Reiza and Hōbi-nyūza,A temporary resolution was reached in the 2nd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} (1862) by making Reiza the upper seat (“Kawabe Shōza Nai-za Chōsho,” dated the first month of that year). and conflicts also occurred between the guild and non-guild members, indicating a transitional state toward the {{jalink|ujiko|Q125562175|ujiko system}} that abolished privileges based on family status.Andō, “Historical Study of Early Modern Miyaza.” The aforementioned “Za Chōsho” is introduced in that book’s source appendix as “Kawabe Shō Documents (4).” Today, October 10 is the annual festival day, and this together with January 1; April 10; July 10; August 18; and December 10—six festivals per year—are referred to as “annual festival days.” === Cultural properties === (The type and date of designation are shown in parentheses after each item.) === Wakayama Prefecture designations === * Main hall (Tangible Cultural Property [structure], June 9, {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 54 [{{jalink|1979|Q2484}}]) * Main hall of the sessha Hachioji Shrine (same) :Both are ikken-sha nagare-zukuri structures with hiwada-buki roofing, decorated throughout with brilliant polychrome, with {{jalink|chigi and katsuogi|Q87220234}} on the ridge. Verandas ({{jalink|ōyuka}}) are provided on three sides—the front and both sides—with {{jalink|gibōshi|Q3105399}} balustrades; side screens ({{jalink|wakishōji}}) are installed; and the rear veranda is omitted. The front of the moya has sliding lattice doors ({{jalink|kōshi-do|Q11017436}}), while the other three sides are white-painted board walls; aside from these, the buildings are decorated in polychrome. From the {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}} it is known that the ridgepole-raising of the main shrine’s main hall was in Kan’ei 11, and that of the sessha main hall predates it, in Kan’ei 1; in addition to excellent carvings on the {{jalink|kibana}} and {{jalink|kamaboko (kaerumata)|Q123117911}} elements, the original form at the time of construction has been well preserved, and they are regarded as representative examples of shrine architecture in the prefecture that retains the legacy of the {{jalink|Momoyama period}}.Interpretive signboard titled “Rikishi Shrine Main Hall / Sessha Hachioji Shrine Main Hall,” dated March 31, Heisei 5, issued jointly by the Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education, Wakayama City Board of Education, and Rikishi Shrine.Architectural Institute of Japan (ed.), “Sōran Nihon no Kenchiku,” Vol. 6-II (Nara & Wakayama), Shinkenchiku-sha, 2002. The sessha main hall is built somewhat larger than the main shrine’s main hall; however, whereas the walls of the sessha’s moya are plain, the main shrine’s main hall has flowers painted on the walls of both side faces. Both were designated in Shōwa 54.{{Cite web|Japanese |author=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkenzo.html |title=Prefecture-designated Cultural Properties / Tangible Cultural Properties / Structures |accessdate=2009-10-25 }} * Kawabe Ōji remains (Historic site, April 1, Shōwa 33 [1958]) :Designated in Shōwa 33 as the “Kawabe Ōji remains,” the ruins of Kawabe Ōji, one of the Kumano 99 Ōji.{{Cite web|Japanese |author=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=Prefecture-designated Cultural Properties / Monuments |accessdate=2009-10-25 }} However, as the Kawabe Ōji remains, Zoku Fudoki identifies Ueno—the former site of Hachioji Shrine—as the location, and since “Wakayama-ken Seiseki” reaffirmed that view, that site is now considered a strong candidate (currently designated a Wakayama City historic site); moreover, there is also a theory that this shrine is the site where Nakamura Ōji was located, and many issues remain regarding the locations of the Ōji on the north bank of the {{jalink|Kinokawa River}} including Kawabe Ōji.{{jalink|Koyama Yasunori|Q11460013}} “Kumano Pilgrimage and the Pilgrimage Roads” (in “Wakayama City History,” Vol. 1, Wakayama City, Heisei 3; “Medieval Wakayama,” Chapter 1, Section 4, Item 1). === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Andō Seiichi, “Historical Study of Early Modern Miyaza,” Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, Shōwa 35 (1960) * Kishū Domain (ed.), “Kii Zoku Fudoki” (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Clergy Supervisory Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, Meiji 43 (1910) * Takaichi Shiyū, “Kii Meisho Zue” 1 (retitled reprint edition by Rekishi Tosho-sha), Rekishi Tosho-sha, Shōwa 45 (1970) * Hashimoto Kankichi, “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” (in Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), “Shikinaisha Survey Report,” Vol. 23, Nankaidō, Kōgakkan University Press, Shōwa 62 [1987], included) * “Wakayama Kenshi,” Vol. 2, Wakayama Prefecture, Taishō 3 (1914) * “Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture” (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), Heibonsha, 1983 (Shōwa 58) ISBN 9784582490312 * “Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place Names,” Vol. 30 (Wakayama Prefecture), Kadokawa Shoten, Shōwa 60 (1985) ISBN 4-04-001300-X * “Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer,” Wakayama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines, Heisei 7 (1995) === Related items === * {{jalink|Kumano Kujūku Ōji (Wakayama City)|Q11370042}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1019 Rikishi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines) {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Rikishi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kumano Hongū Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]

'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '和歌山県田辺市本宮町本宮', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:力侍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11398393}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Rikishi Shrine |Image = [[Image:Rikishi_Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Approach to Rikishi Shrine |Location = {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}} Kawabe, aza Inai 61 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank/status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} claimed shrine (ronsha) / formerly {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} |Founded = Traditionally: mid-{{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} (kokerabuki) |Other name = |Pilgrimage site etc. = |Annual festival = October 10 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Rikishi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Rikishi Shrine'''|力侍神社}} is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kawabe, {{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}}, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}. Beginning with its所在地 of Kawabe, it attracts faith across the area as the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655|general ujigami}} for Ueno, Shima, Konami, and Kusumoto. It stands side by side east–west with Hachioji Shrine, which is a {{jalink|sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}}. There is also a theory that it is the successor shrine to “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” of Muro District, Kii Province in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} ({{jalink|ronsha|Q11064932|disputed Shikinaisha}}). It was formerly a {{jalink|village shrine|Q712534|former village shrine}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|modern shrine rank system}}. The {{jalink|main hall|Q2044994}} is designated a Tangible Cultural Property (structure) of Wakayama Prefecture together with that of Hachioji Shrine. The precincts are designated a Wakayama Prefecture historic site as the remains of {{jalink|Kawabe Ōji|Q11370042|Kawabe Ōji}}, one of the {{jalink|Kumano Kujūku Ōji||Kumano 99 Ōji}}. === Enshrined deities === It enshrines {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Mikoto|Q849412}} and {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|Q3090037}},“Nanki Jinja Roku” and “Meisho Zue” list the enshrined deity as **Asai-hime-no-Mikoto**. and additionally enshrines 13 other kami. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was originally enshrined at Ōyunohara (the former shrine site of {{jalink|Kumano Hongū Taisha|Q705035}}) in {{jalink|Hongū Town (Wakayama Prefecture)|Q2251838|Hongū Town}}, {{jalink|Tanabe City|Q304266}}, Wakayama Prefecture,{{Wiki coordinates dms|33|50|04|N|135|46|28|E|scale:25000}} but it is said that its founding began when, in the mid-{{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, it was transferred to Kuwahata in the aza of Konami, northeast of the present shrine site. “{{jalink|Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964}}” (hereafter abbreviated as “Zoku Fudoki”) argues that the “Jūshii-i-jō Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami” among the 30 local deities listed in “{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Shinmyōchō|Q17210008}}” (hereafter “Kokuchō”) in Nagusa District refers to this shrine, that the “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” listed in Muro District of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō was its predecessor, and that from the deity name and exploits of Tajikarao, the shrine name “{{jalink|rikishi|Q2727289}}” arose, which then changed into “Rikishi” Shrine; however,Although “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” appears in Muro District in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, the Kokuchō for Muro District contains neither that shrine name nor even a similar shrine; thus it argues that “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” was moved from Muro District to Nagusa District after compilation of the Engishiki, and therefore was recorded in the Kokuchō as “Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami” under Nagusa District, and that this was also the predecessor of the present shrine. because it cannot be proven from historical sources, it has been judged to have weak evidentiary basis.Hashimoto, “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine.” According to shrine tradition, after its founding it was transferred to the site of Hachioji Shrine in Ueno, immediately west of Konami,{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|16|06.0|N|135|15|11.7|E|scale:25000}} and later, in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kan’ei|Q1192913}} (March {{jalink|1626|Q6831}}) in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, it was moved together with Hachioji Shrine to the present location by divine oracle; Hachioji Shrine came to be enshrined in parallel as a sessha on the east side (to the right as you face it).Zoku Fudoki records a similar tradition. However, as noted below, the raising of the ridgepole of the present main hall of Hachioji Shrine was in the 1st year of Kan’ei. “Meisho Zue” also records the tradition that the transfer to the present site was due to a divine oracle, but dates it later, to the Hōei era (early 18th century). Since then it was regarded as the {{jalink|ubusuna-gami|Q1392097}} of four villages—Kawabe, Kusumoto, Shima, and Konami. According to Zoku Fudoki, a subtemple of {{jalink|Ninna-ji|Q1202871}} called Enkyōzan Sannō-in, a branch temple of {{jalink|Shinjō-in|Q131226873}}, was placed in the precincts as the shrine’s {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755|bettō jingū-ji}} (later abolished). In the 6th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in the 43rd year ({{jalink|1910|Q2075}}) it merged nearby shrines. A small shrine in the former Konami Village thought to be a remnant shrine of the original enshrinement site was also merged at that time,“Wakayama Kenshi.” and likewise Kusumoto Shrine in Kusumoto, regarded as the former site of Nakamura Ōji (one of the 99 Ōji), and the Hachioji shrine in Ueno (a small shrine remaining at the former enshrinement site in Ueno, regarded as the remains of Kawabe Ōji) were also merged. === Shrine buildings === The main hall is an {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684|ikken-sha nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{jalink|kokerabuki||kokerabuki}} roofing, and is a Wakayama Prefecture-designated Tangible Cultural Property (see the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section below). === Shrines within the precincts === ;Sessha: Hachioji Shrine :Today it enshrines {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|Q462555}}, the {{jalink|Five male and three female deities}}, {{jalink|Yachimatahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Yachimatahime-no-Mikoto}} as principal deities, with {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508}} and 11 other kami enshrined additionally. As noted above, it was formerly enshrined in Ueno; citing the “Kan’ei-ki,”A compilation of reports ordered by the {{jalink|Kishū Tokugawa clan|Q11605046}} after entering the domain to investigate “shrines and temples, old traditions and historic sites” within Kii Province, compiled over roughly 50 years from Kan’ei to Kanbun. The compilation appears to have been carried out multiple times; “Kan’ei-ki” refers to the portion compiled in the Kan’ei era (Zoku Fudoki). Zoku Fudoki states that it was founded in the {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} era (early 9th century), served as the ubusuna-gami for seven villages (Kawabe, Kusumoto, Shima, Konami, Ueno, Bessho, and Ochiai), and held more than 15 {{jalink|chō (unit)|Q1185752}} of shrine land, but that all shrine lands were confiscated during {{jalink|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550}}’s {{jalink|Kishū campaign|Q10855721|subjugation of Kishū}} in the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} period and that old documents were lost at that time. Zoku Fudoki itself further argues: “Upon consideration, this shrine is Kawabe Ōji as recorded in the ‘Kumano Gokō-ki,’“Gotoba-in Kumano Gokō-ki.” The record of Emperor Go-Toba’s Kumano pilgrimage in the 1st year of Kennin ({{jalink|1201|Q5225}}), described in {{jalink|Fujiwara no Teika|Q378425}}’s diary “{{jalink|Meigetsuki|Q3007406}}.” Also called “Kumano Michi no Aida Guki.” and this is the Hachioji shrine mentioned by the monk {{jalink|Shinkei|Q1770663}} in the afterword of ‘Sasamegoto.’”Shinkei was a mid-{{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} monk from Tai-no-shō, which included Kawabe Village, and was also active as a renga poet. His work “Sasamegoto” is a treatise on renga; its afterword states that after returning home for the first time in 55 years, he stayed in seclusion at Kawabe Ōji and wrote it. The main hall, like that of the main shrine, is also a prefecture-designated Tangible Cultural Property (see the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section below). ;Massha: Kusunoki Shrine :It enshrines Kusunoki Daimyōjin and stands east of Hachioji Shrine within the tamagaki fence. It was originally enshrined at Kusumoto, aza Minami-no-kuchi in Wakayama City,{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|15|37.5|N|135|15|30.9|E|scale:25000}} and is seen as “Jūshii-i-jō Kusumoto Ōkami” in the Kokuchō,“Nanki Jinja Roku,” “Meisho Zue,” and Zoku Fudoki. Like “Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami,” “Kusumoto Ōkami” is among the 30 local deities of Nagusa District. and in early modern times it was called “Kusumoto Shrine” or “Kusumoto Daimyōjin.” Tradition holds that it was founded by {{jalink|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979}} in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} (786), after he exterminated a giant spider living in Kusumoto; it was made an unranked shrine in Meiji 6, but in Meiji 43 it was merged into Rikishi Shrine in the form of a transfer of enshrinement. Regarding this giant spider, “{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Meisho Zue}}” (first volume published in the 8th year of {{jalink|Bunka (era)|Q1373146|Bunka}} [{{jalink|1811|Q6928}}]; hereafter “Meisho Zue”) says that long ago in Kusumoto a huge {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}} towered, and a giant spider there spun a web from the camphor tree to Wasa-yama (present {{jalink|Takatsumi-yama}}),{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|14|05.31|N|135|15|40.8|E|scale:25000}} troubling the people; therefore, in the {{jalink|Daidō (Japan)|Q1373191|Daidō}} era (early 9th century), Tamura Shōgun (Tamuramaro), having received an {{jalink|imperial command}}, exterminated it and felled the camphor tree as well, and from the stump camphor trees grew in clusters, forming a forest; it further records that at that time (early 19th century) there was in fact a stump around 30 {{jalink|hiro|Q11458676}} (about 50 {{jalink|meters|Q11573}}) in circumference, thought to be several thousand years old. Zoku Fudoki identifies Kusumoto Shrine with “{{jalink|Nakamura Ōji site|Q135351770}}” among the 99 Ōji, and this is considered the current prevailing theory, though as noted below there are dissenting views (see “{{jalink|#Historic site Kawabe Ōji remains||Historic site Kawabe Ōji remains}}” in the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section). In addition, {{jalink|Sugihara Yasushige}} (the “{{jalink|Nanki Jinja Roku}}” of {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 3 [1746]) and “Meisho Zue” list the enshrined deity as {{jalink|Kumano Kusubi|Q11298796|Kumano Kusubi-no-Mikoto}}. In addition, there are subsidiary shrines: Ushidaki-sha (within the tamagaki fence), Inari-sha, and Myōken-sha. === Rituals === In early modern times, as noted above, a bettō temple was established, and the four “ubugo” villages also organized a {{jalink|miyaza|Q11454763}} that appears to date back to the medieval period, overseeing rituals, shrine construction, rebuilding, and transfers of the deity. The miyaza was operated around the “bandō” (said to preserve the name of the {{jalink|bandō|Q11578792}} placed in medieval {{jalink|shōen|Q1550557}} estates). The bandō formed a group called “Tsuitachi-kō,” holding meetings on the first day of the 1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, and 12th lunar months, where they made decisions broadly covering village governance as well as shrine management. Members other than the bandō were called “Satamori (Sato-no-mori)” and also “Hiraza.” At first these roles were filled based on old family rank and tradition supported by economic advantage, but as some lineages died out and others declined with socio-economic development, from the mid–early modern period onward new farmers were incorporated, distinguishing old lineages as “Reiza” and new entrants as “Hōbi-nyūza.” They were called “hōbi” mainly because contributions to the guild were required, and as this shows, from the mid-period onward finances became strained; by the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} era they even suspended the Tsuitachi-kō to save costs and reformed long-standing practices to stabilize finances, such as lending saved funds and using the interest as operating capital. Along with this, conflicts arose over seating order between Reiza and Hōbi-nyūza,A temporary resolution was reached in the 2nd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} (1862) by making Reiza the upper seat (“Kawabe Shōza Nai-za Chōsho,” dated the first month of that year). and conflicts also occurred between the guild and non-guild members, indicating a transitional state toward the {{jalink|ujiko|Q125562175|ujiko system}} that abolished privileges based on family status.Andō, “Historical Study of Early Modern Miyaza.” The aforementioned “Za Chōsho” is introduced in that book’s source appendix as “Kawabe Shō Documents (4).” Today, October 10 is the annual festival day, and this together with January 1; April 10; July 10; August 18; and December 10—six festivals per year—are referred to as “annual festival days.” === Cultural properties === (The type and date of designation are shown in parentheses after each item.) === Wakayama Prefecture designations === * Main hall (Tangible Cultural Property [structure], June 9, {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 54 [{{jalink|1979|Q2484}}]) * Main hall of the sessha Hachioji Shrine (same) :Both are ikken-sha nagare-zukuri structures with hiwada-buki roofing, decorated throughout with brilliant polychrome, with {{jalink|chigi and katsuogi|Q87220234}} on the ridge. Verandas ({{jalink|ōyuka}}) are provided on three sides—the front and both sides—with {{jalink|gibōshi|Q3105399}} balustrades; side screens ({{jalink|wakishōji}}) are installed; and the rear veranda is omitted. The front of the moya has sliding lattice doors ({{jalink|kōshi-do|Q11017436}}), while the other three sides are white-painted board walls; aside from these, the buildings are decorated in polychrome. From the {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}} it is known that the ridgepole-raising of the main shrine’s main hall was in Kan’ei 11, and that of the sessha main hall predates it, in Kan’ei 1; in addition to excellent carvings on the {{jalink|kibana}} and {{jalink|kamaboko (kaerumata)|Q123117911}} elements, the original form at the time of construction has been well preserved, and they are regarded as representative examples of shrine architecture in the prefecture that retains the legacy of the {{jalink|Momoyama period}}.Interpretive signboard titled “Rikishi Shrine Main Hall / Sessha Hachioji Shrine Main Hall,” dated March 31, Heisei 5, issued jointly by the Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education, Wakayama City Board of Education, and Rikishi Shrine.Architectural Institute of Japan (ed.), “Sōran Nihon no Kenchiku,” Vol. 6-II (Nara & Wakayama), Shinkenchiku-sha, 2002. The sessha main hall is built somewhat larger than the main shrine’s main hall; however, whereas the walls of the sessha’s moya are plain, the main shrine’s main hall has flowers painted on the walls of both side faces. Both were designated in Shōwa 54.{{Cite web|Japanese |author=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkenzo.html |title=Prefecture-designated Cultural Properties / Tangible Cultural Properties / Structures |accessdate=2009-10-25 }} * Kawabe Ōji remains (Historic site, April 1, Shōwa 33 [1958]) :Designated in Shōwa 33 as the “Kawabe Ōji remains,” the ruins of Kawabe Ōji, one of the Kumano 99 Ōji.{{Cite web|Japanese |author=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=Prefecture-designated Cultural Properties / Monuments |accessdate=2009-10-25 }} However, as the Kawabe Ōji remains, Zoku Fudoki identifies Ueno—the former site of Hachioji Shrine—as the location, and since “Wakayama-ken Seiseki” reaffirmed that view, that site is now considered a strong candidate (currently designated a Wakayama City historic site); moreover, there is also a theory that this shrine is the site where Nakamura Ōji was located, and many issues remain regarding the locations of the Ōji on the north bank of the {{jalink|Kinokawa River}} including Kawabe Ōji.{{jalink|Koyama Yasunori|Q11460013}} “Kumano Pilgrimage and the Pilgrimage Roads” (in “Wakayama City History,” Vol. 1, Wakayama City, Heisei 3; “Medieval Wakayama,” Chapter 1, Section 4, Item 1). === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Andō Seiichi, “Historical Study of Early Modern Miyaza,” Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, Shōwa 35 (1960) * Kishū Domain (ed.), “Kii Zoku Fudoki” (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Clergy Supervisory Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, Meiji 43 (1910) * Takaichi Shiyū, “Kii Meisho Zue” 1 (retitled reprint edition by Rekishi Tosho-sha), Rekishi Tosho-sha, Shōwa 45 (1970) * Hashimoto Kankichi, “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” (in Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), “Shikinaisha Survey Report,” Vol. 23, Nankaidō, Kōgakkan University Press, Shōwa 62 [1987], included) * “Wakayama Kenshi,” Vol. 2, Wakayama Prefecture, Taishō 3 (1914) * “Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture” (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), Heibonsha, 1983 (Shōwa 58) ISBN 9784582490312 * “Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place Names,” Vol. 30 (Wakayama Prefecture), Kadokawa Shoten, Shōwa 60 (1985) ISBN 4-04-001300-X * “Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer,” Wakayama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines, Heisei 7 (1995) === Related items === * {{jalink|Kumano Kujūku Ōji (Wakayama City)|Q11370042}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1019 Rikishi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines) {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Rikishi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Rikishi Shrine]][[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kumano Hongū Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]

'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '和歌山県田辺市本宮町本宮', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:力侍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11398393}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Rikishi Shrine |Image = [[Image:Rikishi_Shrine.JPG|270px]]
Approach to Rikishi Shrine |Location = {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}} Kawabe, aza Inai 61 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank/status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} claimed shrine (ronsha) / formerly {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}} |Founded = Traditionally: mid-{{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} |Main hall = Ikken-sha {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} (kokerabuki) |Other name = |Pilgrimage site etc. = |Annual festival = October 10 |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Rikishi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Rikishi Shrine'''|力侍神社}} is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kawabe, {{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}}, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}. Beginning with its所在地 of Kawabe, it attracts faith across the area as the {{jalink|ujigami|Q767655|general ujigami}} for Ueno, Shima, Konami, and Kusumoto. It stands side by side east–west with Hachioji Shrine, which is a {{jalink|sessha|Q1071160|subsidiary shrine}}. There is also a theory that it is the successor shrine to “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” of Muro District, Kii Province in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} ({{jalink|ronsha|Q11064932|disputed Shikinaisha}}). It was formerly a {{jalink|village shrine|Q712534|former village shrine}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|modern shrine rank system}}. The {{jalink|main hall|Q2044994}} is designated a Tangible Cultural Property (structure) of Wakayama Prefecture together with that of Hachioji Shrine. The precincts are designated a Wakayama Prefecture historic site as the remains of {{jalink|Kawabe Ōji|Q11370042|Kawabe Ōji}}, one of the {{jalink|Kumano Kujūku Ōji||Kumano 99 Ōji}}. === Enshrined deities === It enshrines {{jalink|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Mikoto|Q849412}} and {{jalink|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto|Q3090037}},“Nanki Jinja Roku” and “Meisho Zue” list the enshrined deity as **Asai-hime-no-Mikoto**. and additionally enshrines 13 other kami. === History === According to shrine tradition, it was originally enshrined at Ōyunohara (the former shrine site of {{jalink|Kumano Hongū Taisha|Q705035}}) in {{jalink|Hongū Town (Wakayama Prefecture)|Q2251838|Hongū Town}}, {{jalink|Tanabe City|Q304266}}, Wakayama Prefecture,{{Wiki coordinates dms|33|50|04|N|135|46|28|E|scale:25000}} but it is said that its founding began when, in the mid-{{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, it was transferred to Kuwahata in the aza of Konami, northeast of the present shrine site. “{{jalink|Kii Zoku Fudoki|Q11604964}}” (hereafter abbreviated as “Zoku Fudoki”) argues that the “Jūshii-i-jō Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami” among the 30 local deities listed in “{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Shinmyōchō|Q17210008}}” (hereafter “Kokuchō”) in Nagusa District refers to this shrine, that the “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” listed in Muro District of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō was its predecessor, and that from the deity name and exploits of Tajikarao, the shrine name “{{jalink|rikishi|Q2727289}}” arose, which then changed into “Rikishi” Shrine; however,Although “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” appears in Muro District in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, the Kokuchō for Muro District contains neither that shrine name nor even a similar shrine; thus it argues that “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” was moved from Muro District to Nagusa District after compilation of the Engishiki, and therefore was recorded in the Kokuchō as “Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami” under Nagusa District, and that this was also the predecessor of the present shrine. because it cannot be proven from historical sources, it has been judged to have weak evidentiary basis.Hashimoto, “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine.” According to shrine tradition, after its founding it was transferred to the site of Hachioji Shrine in Ueno, immediately west of Konami,{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|16|06.0|N|135|15|11.7|E|scale:25000}} and later, in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kan’ei|Q1192913}} (March {{jalink|1626|Q6831}}) in the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, it was moved together with Hachioji Shrine to the present location by divine oracle; Hachioji Shrine came to be enshrined in parallel as a sessha on the east side (to the right as you face it).Zoku Fudoki records a similar tradition. However, as noted below, the raising of the ridgepole of the present main hall of Hachioji Shrine was in the 1st year of Kan’ei. “Meisho Zue” also records the tradition that the transfer to the present site was due to a divine oracle, but dates it later, to the Hōei era (early 18th century). Since then it was regarded as the {{jalink|ubusuna-gami|Q1392097}} of four villages—Kawabe, Kusumoto, Shima, and Konami. According to Zoku Fudoki, a subtemple of {{jalink|Ninna-ji|Q1202871}} called Enkyōzan Sannō-in, a branch temple of {{jalink|Shinjō-in|Q131226873}}, was placed in the precincts as the shrine’s {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755|bettō jingū-ji}} (later abolished). In the 6th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} (1873) it was ranked as a village shrine, and in the 43rd year ({{jalink|1910|Q2075}}) it merged nearby shrines. A small shrine in the former Konami Village thought to be a remnant shrine of the original enshrinement site was also merged at that time,“Wakayama Kenshi.” and likewise Kusumoto Shrine in Kusumoto, regarded as the former site of Nakamura Ōji (one of the 99 Ōji), and the Hachioji shrine in Ueno (a small shrine remaining at the former enshrinement site in Ueno, regarded as the remains of Kawabe Ōji) were also merged. === Shrine buildings === The main hall is an {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684|ikken-sha nagare-zukuri}} structure with {{jalink|kokerabuki||kokerabuki}} roofing, and is a Wakayama Prefecture-designated Tangible Cultural Property (see the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section below). === Shrines within the precincts === ;Sessha: Hachioji Shrine :Today it enshrines {{jalink|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|Q462555}}, the {{jalink|Five male and three female deities}}, {{jalink|Yachimatahiko-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Yachimatahime-no-Mikoto}} as principal deities, with {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|Q2564508}} and 11 other kami enshrined additionally. As noted above, it was formerly enshrined in Ueno; citing the “Kan’ei-ki,”A compilation of reports ordered by the {{jalink|Kishū Tokugawa clan|Q11605046}} after entering the domain to investigate “shrines and temples, old traditions and historic sites” within Kii Province, compiled over roughly 50 years from Kan’ei to Kanbun. The compilation appears to have been carried out multiple times; “Kan’ei-ki” refers to the portion compiled in the Kan’ei era (Zoku Fudoki). Zoku Fudoki states that it was founded in the {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} era (early 9th century), served as the ubusuna-gami for seven villages (Kawabe, Kusumoto, Shima, Konami, Ueno, Bessho, and Ochiai), and held more than 15 {{jalink|chō (unit)|Q1185752}} of shrine land, but that all shrine lands were confiscated during {{jalink|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550}}’s {{jalink|Kishū campaign|Q10855721|subjugation of Kishū}} in the {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} period and that old documents were lost at that time. Zoku Fudoki itself further argues: “Upon consideration, this shrine is Kawabe Ōji as recorded in the ‘Kumano Gokō-ki,’“Gotoba-in Kumano Gokō-ki.” The record of Emperor Go-Toba’s Kumano pilgrimage in the 1st year of Kennin ({{jalink|1201|Q5225}}), described in {{jalink|Fujiwara no Teika|Q378425}}’s diary “{{jalink|Meigetsuki|Q3007406}}.” Also called “Kumano Michi no Aida Guki.” and this is the Hachioji shrine mentioned by the monk {{jalink|Shinkei|Q1770663}} in the afterword of ‘Sasamegoto.’”Shinkei was a mid-{{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} monk from Tai-no-shō, which included Kawabe Village, and was also active as a renga poet. His work “Sasamegoto” is a treatise on renga; its afterword states that after returning home for the first time in 55 years, he stayed in seclusion at Kawabe Ōji and wrote it. The main hall, like that of the main shrine, is also a prefecture-designated Tangible Cultural Property (see the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section below). ;Massha: Kusunoki Shrine :It enshrines Kusunoki Daimyōjin and stands east of Hachioji Shrine within the tamagaki fence. It was originally enshrined at Kusumoto, aza Minami-no-kuchi in Wakayama City,{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|15|37.5|N|135|15|30.9|E|scale:25000}} and is seen as “Jūshii-i-jō Kusumoto Ōkami” in the Kokuchō,“Nanki Jinja Roku,” “Meisho Zue,” and Zoku Fudoki. Like “Ame-no-Tajikarao no Kami,” “Kusumoto Ōkami” is among the 30 local deities of Nagusa District. and in early modern times it was called “Kusumoto Shrine” or “Kusumoto Daimyōjin.” Tradition holds that it was founded by {{jalink|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Q941979}} in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enryaku|Q1373176}} (786), after he exterminated a giant spider living in Kusumoto; it was made an unranked shrine in Meiji 6, but in Meiji 43 it was merged into Rikishi Shrine in the form of a transfer of enshrinement. Regarding this giant spider, “{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Meisho Zue}}” (first volume published in the 8th year of {{jalink|Bunka (era)|Q1373146|Bunka}} [{{jalink|1811|Q6928}}]; hereafter “Meisho Zue”) says that long ago in Kusumoto a huge {{jalink|camphor tree|Q158722}} towered, and a giant spider there spun a web from the camphor tree to Wasa-yama (present {{jalink|Takatsumi-yama}}),{{Wiki coordinates dms|34|14|05.31|N|135|15|40.8|E|scale:25000}} troubling the people; therefore, in the {{jalink|Daidō (Japan)|Q1373191|Daidō}} era (early 9th century), Tamura Shōgun (Tamuramaro), having received an {{jalink|imperial command}}, exterminated it and felled the camphor tree as well, and from the stump camphor trees grew in clusters, forming a forest; it further records that at that time (early 19th century) there was in fact a stump around 30 {{jalink|hiro|Q11458676}} (about 50 {{jalink|meters|Q11573}}) in circumference, thought to be several thousand years old. Zoku Fudoki identifies Kusumoto Shrine with “{{jalink|Nakamura Ōji site|Q135351770}}” among the 99 Ōji, and this is considered the current prevailing theory, though as noted below there are dissenting views (see “{{jalink|#Historic site Kawabe Ōji remains||Historic site Kawabe Ōji remains}}” in the “{{jalink|#Cultural properties||Cultural properties}}” section). In addition, {{jalink|Sugihara Yasushige}} (the “{{jalink|Nanki Jinja Roku}}” of {{jalink|Enkyō|Q1183728}} 3 [1746]) and “Meisho Zue” list the enshrined deity as {{jalink|Kumano Kusubi|Q11298796|Kumano Kusubi-no-Mikoto}}. In addition, there are subsidiary shrines: Ushidaki-sha (within the tamagaki fence), Inari-sha, and Myōken-sha. === Rituals === In early modern times, as noted above, a bettō temple was established, and the four “ubugo” villages also organized a {{jalink|miyaza|Q11454763}} that appears to date back to the medieval period, overseeing rituals, shrine construction, rebuilding, and transfers of the deity. The miyaza was operated around the “bandō” (said to preserve the name of the {{jalink|bandō|Q11578792}} placed in medieval {{jalink|shōen|Q1550557}} estates). The bandō formed a group called “Tsuitachi-kō,” holding meetings on the first day of the 1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, and 12th lunar months, where they made decisions broadly covering village governance as well as shrine management. Members other than the bandō were called “Satamori (Sato-no-mori)” and also “Hiraza.” At first these roles were filled based on old family rank and tradition supported by economic advantage, but as some lineages died out and others declined with socio-economic development, from the mid–early modern period onward new farmers were incorporated, distinguishing old lineages as “Reiza” and new entrants as “Hōbi-nyūza.” They were called “hōbi” mainly because contributions to the guild were required, and as this shows, from the mid-period onward finances became strained; by the {{jalink|Bakumatsu|Q804558}} era they even suspended the Tsuitachi-kō to save costs and reformed long-standing practices to stabilize finances, such as lending saved funds and using the interest as operating capital. Along with this, conflicts arose over seating order between Reiza and Hōbi-nyūza,A temporary resolution was reached in the 2nd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} (1862) by making Reiza the upper seat (“Kawabe Shōza Nai-za Chōsho,” dated the first month of that year). and conflicts also occurred between the guild and non-guild members, indicating a transitional state toward the {{jalink|ujiko|Q125562175|ujiko system}} that abolished privileges based on family status.Andō, “Historical Study of Early Modern Miyaza.” The aforementioned “Za Chōsho” is introduced in that book’s source appendix as “Kawabe Shō Documents (4).” Today, October 10 is the annual festival day, and this together with January 1; April 10; July 10; August 18; and December 10—six festivals per year—are referred to as “annual festival days.” === Cultural properties === (The type and date of designation are shown in parentheses after each item.) === Wakayama Prefecture designations === * Main hall (Tangible Cultural Property [structure], June 9, {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 54 [{{jalink|1979|Q2484}}]) * Main hall of the sessha Hachioji Shrine (same) :Both are ikken-sha nagare-zukuri structures with hiwada-buki roofing, decorated throughout with brilliant polychrome, with {{jalink|chigi and katsuogi|Q87220234}} on the ridge. Verandas ({{jalink|ōyuka}}) are provided on three sides—the front and both sides—with {{jalink|gibōshi|Q3105399}} balustrades; side screens ({{jalink|wakishōji}}) are installed; and the rear veranda is omitted. The front of the moya has sliding lattice doors ({{jalink|kōshi-do|Q11017436}}), while the other three sides are white-painted board walls; aside from these, the buildings are decorated in polychrome. From the {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}} it is known that the ridgepole-raising of the main shrine’s main hall was in Kan’ei 11, and that of the sessha main hall predates it, in Kan’ei 1; in addition to excellent carvings on the {{jalink|kibana}} and {{jalink|kamaboko (kaerumata)|Q123117911}} elements, the original form at the time of construction has been well preserved, and they are regarded as representative examples of shrine architecture in the prefecture that retains the legacy of the {{jalink|Momoyama period}}.Interpretive signboard titled “Rikishi Shrine Main Hall / Sessha Hachioji Shrine Main Hall,” dated March 31, Heisei 5, issued jointly by the Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education, Wakayama City Board of Education, and Rikishi Shrine.Architectural Institute of Japan (ed.), “Sōran Nihon no Kenchiku,” Vol. 6-II (Nara & Wakayama), Shinkenchiku-sha, 2002. The sessha main hall is built somewhat larger than the main shrine’s main hall; however, whereas the walls of the sessha’s moya are plain, the main shrine’s main hall has flowers painted on the walls of both side faces. Both were designated in Shōwa 54.{{Cite web|Japanese |author=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkenzo.html |title=Prefecture-designated Cultural Properties / Tangible Cultural Properties / Structures |accessdate=2009-10-25 }} * Kawabe Ōji remains (Historic site, April 1, Shōwa 33 [1958]) :Designated in Shōwa 33 as the “Kawabe Ōji remains,” the ruins of Kawabe Ōji, one of the Kumano 99 Ōji.{{Cite web|Japanese |author=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=Prefecture-designated Cultural Properties / Monuments |accessdate=2009-10-25 }} However, as the Kawabe Ōji remains, Zoku Fudoki identifies Ueno—the former site of Hachioji Shrine—as the location, and since “Wakayama-ken Seiseki” reaffirmed that view, that site is now considered a strong candidate (currently designated a Wakayama City historic site); moreover, there is also a theory that this shrine is the site where Nakamura Ōji was located, and many issues remain regarding the locations of the Ōji on the north bank of the {{jalink|Kinokawa River}} including Kawabe Ōji.{{jalink|Koyama Yasunori|Q11460013}} “Kumano Pilgrimage and the Pilgrimage Roads” (in “Wakayama City History,” Vol. 1, Wakayama City, Heisei 3; “Medieval Wakayama,” Chapter 1, Section 4, Item 1). === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Andō Seiichi, “Historical Study of Early Modern Miyaza,” Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, Shōwa 35 (1960) * Kishū Domain (ed.), “Kii Zoku Fudoki” (reprinted by Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Clergy Supervisory Office), Teikoku Chihō Gyōsei Gakkai Shuppanbu, Meiji 43 (1910) * Takaichi Shiyū, “Kii Meisho Zue” 1 (retitled reprint edition by Rekishi Tosho-sha), Rekishi Tosho-sha, Shōwa 45 (1970) * Hashimoto Kankichi, “Ame-no-Tajikarao Shrine” (in Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), “Shikinaisha Survey Report,” Vol. 23, Nankaidō, Kōgakkan University Press, Shōwa 62 [1987], included) * “Wakayama Kenshi,” Vol. 2, Wakayama Prefecture, Taishō 3 (1914) * “Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture” (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31), Heibonsha, 1983 (Shōwa 58) ISBN 9784582490312 * “Kadokawa Dictionary of Japanese Place Names,” Vol. 30 (Wakayama Prefecture), Kadokawa Shoten, Shōwa 60 (1985) ISBN 4-04-001300-X * “Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer,” Wakayama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines, Heisei 7 (1995) === Related items === * {{jalink|Kumano Kujūku Ōji (Wakayama City)|Q11370042}} === External links === * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1019 Rikishi Shrine] (Wakayama Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines) {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Rikishi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Rikishi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kumano Hongū Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kii Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:和歌山市の神社]] [[Category:紀伊国の式内社]] [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:和歌山県の旧村社]]
